tweets.text:
$MSTR 

My biggest hope is that Saylor has the humility and discernment to deleverage Strategy as much as he can when the market hunts him.

e.g. as there's uncertainty in prefs and they see a massive discount in a scenario when BTC / MSTR are also falling, be willing to sell enough Bitcoin to take advantage of that uncertainty and buy back as much discounted debt and obligations as he can rather than trying to ride out the storm and finding himself in an even worse position

Make Bitcoin great again.

tweets.text:
Again - the key thing here is for him to find a way to sustainably deleverage.

I don’t want Strategy to die and don’t think it has to in order for BTC to bounce back.

Every new decision they make will objectively impact my view of where they are now.

The one thing that will not help their case in my eyes is adding more leverage to try and Martindale their way out of the situation.

thread_tweets.text:
the issue with adding more debt via convertibles is their current convertibles are already so OTM that there is an extremely high likelihood that ~$6B of debt will also have to be paid off well before the end of 2028 (similar to the last $1.38B for $1.5B of debt move)...

so one way or another - you're now raising debt to pay off debt

vs. previously they were raising debt via convertibles to stack more BTC

paying high yield on a non-yield bearing asset for a company w/ no real significant revenue is a losing game for everyone at the bottom

tweets.text:
intensity is a learned skill, as much as anything else is. The weird current trend that mixes super low volume with 'science' based lifting that negates heavy compounds imo is a pretty great way for beginners to spin their wheels by not training with enough stimulus to drive growth via either volume, intensity or major body movement patterns (which probs falls slightly under intensity in and of itself)

I like all these approaches in isolation, or with nuance, but no substitute for hard work and that's what you should learn early on in your lifting career (not to mention higher volume gives you more reps under the bar to get proficient at movements over time)
tweets.full_text:

tweets.text:
My late teens early 20’s were wild times

Thankful to have survived it
tweets.full_text:
My late teens early 20’s were wild times

Thankful to have survived it

tweets.text: When I was 18 I got sucker punched and jumped outside a store by a group of black kids for no reason just wrong place wrong time. They used to call it the knock out game but thankfully I didn’t get knocked out. Was able to fight my way out of the crowd and run while watching my “friends” who brought me to the store drive off in fear. My jaw is still fucked up from that night but since then I’ve never thought to myself I wish I had a gun or a knife that night so I coulda killed somebody

thread_tweets.text: I did defend myself I survived, having a knife and using it probably would have reduced the probably of survival when I think about it due to the numbers and introducing a deadly weapon into the conflict. I think you’re mistaking the point I’m trying to make here. Kids that bring knives to a high school track meet in the suburbs isn’t normal that’s somebody who wants to use that knife and did. If I used a knife in my situation it would be justified but I don’t look back and wish I did/had because killing is only a last resort
